coredata.dc:title: The photo-physicochemical properties and in vitro photodynamic therapy activity of differently substituted-zinc (II)-phthalocyanines and graphene quantum dots conjugates on MCF7 breast cancer cell line
coredata.dc:creator: Nene, Lindokuhle C.
Managa, Muthumuni E.
Oluwole, David O.
Mafukidze, Donovan M.
Sindelo, Azole
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Several differently substituted Zn(II) phthalocyanines (ZnPcs) were prepared and conjugated to GQDs. The photophysical properties were determined for both the Pcs and their respective conjugates including the fluorescence/triplet quantum yields and lifetimes as well as the singlet oxygen generating abilities. Upon conjugation to GQDs, the fluorescence of the Pcs decreased (insignificant decrease in some cases), with an increase in the triplet quantum yields. However, the singlet quantum yields of the Pcs in the conjugates did not show an increase with the increase in the triplet quantum yields, this is suspected to be due to the screening effect. The cytotoxicity of the complexes in vitro decreased upon conjugation, as a result of the reduced actual number of Pcs units provided in the conjugate for therapy. Upon introduction of cationic charges, the photodynamic therapy activity of the complexes increased.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc phthalocyanine
Graphene quantum dots
Singlet oxygen quantum yield
Photodynamic therapy
MCF7 cancer cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quaternization of complex 4 to form 5, Scheme 2
Synthesis and structural characterisation of Pcs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV-vis spectra of A) metal free 2,9,16,23-tetrakis-(2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenoxy)-phthalocyanine, (H2DTBPc) and complex 1 in chloroform, B) complexes 4 and 5 in DMSO, and complex 5 in water, and C) complex 2 and 2-GQDs in DMSO.

The TEM images of A) GQDs in ethanol scale bar: 100 nm and B) 1-GQDs in methanol at 10X mag, scale bar: 200 nm. The DLS graphs showing the average size estimations of C) GQDs in water and D) 1-GQDs in DMSO.
The Raman spectrum of GQDs and 4-GQDs showing the D and G bands.

A) The UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of 1 in DMSO, λexc = 610 nm, and B) the emission spectrum of GQDs in DMSO, the absorption and emission spectra of 2 in 2-GQDs upon excitation at 354 nm in DMSO respectively. Insert: Emission spectrum of Pc alone at λexc 354 nm in DMSO.
The TCSPC Fluorescence decay curve of 1 in DMSO when excited at 679 nm.
The triplet decay curve of 1-GQDs when excited at 677 nm in DMSO.
Changes in the absorption spectra of DPBF with irradiation time in the presence of complex 1 in DMSO. (inset: change in absorbance of DPBF vs irradiation time (s)).
Histograms showing the MCF-7 cells cell viability (%) in A) dark and B) PDT by 4, 4-GQDs and 5-GQDs in vitro, n = 3. Control = 1% DMSO in cultured media.

Cytograms of MCF-7 cells on clear-bottom-96-well plates in 1% DMSO-DMEM inoculated with 100 μg/mL of 5-GQDs incubated at 37 °C at 5% CO2, A) when incubated in the dark and B) irradiated for 5 min. Magnification: 10×, Scale bar: 200 μm.
Method of π-π conjugating phthalocyanines on to graphene quantum dots.
A Summary of the absorption, emission, excitation and structural characteristics of the Pc and Pc-GQDs in DMSO.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: TW = this work. References in square brackets.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of the photo-physicochemical properties of the Pcs, GQDs and the Pcs-GQDs complexes in DMSO.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in round brackets are for excitation at the wavelength where GQDs absorb.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of PDT activity of 100 μg/mL Pc and Pc-GQDs on MCF-7 in vitro, n = 3.

head.ce:title: The photo-physicochemical properties and in vitro photodynamic therapy activity of differently substituted-zinc (II)-phthalocyanines and graphene quantum dots conjugates on MCF7 breast cancer cell line

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Grahamstown 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Several differently substituted Zn(II) phthalocyanines (ZnPcs) were prepared and conjugated to GQDs. The photophysical properties were determined for both the Pcs and their respective conjugates including the fluorescence/triplet quantum yields and lifetimes as well as the singlet oxygen generating abilities. Upon conjugation to GQDs, the fluorescence of the Pcs decreased (insignificant decrease in some cases), with an increase in the triplet quantum yields. However, the singlet quantum yields of the Pcs in the conjugates did not show an increase with the increase in the triplet quantum yields, this is suspected to be due to the screening effect. The cytotoxicity of the complexes in vitro decreased upon conjugation, as a result of the reduced actual number of Pcs units provided in the conjugate for therapy. Upon introduction of cationic charges, the photodynamic therapy activity of the complexes increased.

ce:section.ce:para: Conventional cancer treatment methodologies such as chemotherapy and radiation therapies have a major drawback in that they lack specificity to cancer cells [1,2]. The initiation of cytotoxicity for some of these methodologies is uncontrollable, resulting in invasiveness [3]. A relatively new methodology, photodynamic therapy (PDT), is a non-invasive and promising alternative which seeks to address these drawbacks [4]. The mechanism of action in PDT is based on the synergistic activity of light, a photoactive compound referred to as a photosensitizer, and ground state molecular oxygen to generate cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) [4,5]. Several photosensitizer compounds have been designed and studied for PDT including porphyrins, chlorins, boron dipyrromethene dyes and phthalocyanines [6–9].

Phthalocyanines (Pcs) are a group of recent generation photosensitizers which possess impressive photo-physicochemical properties which are attractive for applications such as in PDT [10–12]. Although Pcs advocate for controllable therapy in PDT, they do however, lack specificity to cancer cells [13]. For this reason, the development of delivery systems for Pcs to cancer cells is vital. Nanoparticles (NPs) have generally earned a great deal of interest in medicinal research, particularly as delivery vectors for therapeutics in oncology [14]. Several Pcs have been linked to nanoparticles such as gold [15,16] and semiconductor quantum dots for PDT [17]. However, there have been no PDT studies on the conjugates of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Pcs, despite the fact that GQDs are well known PDT agents [18].

GQDs are a group of fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials which have demonstrated applicability across biological, medicinal and industrial sectors [19–21]. Structurally, GQDs appear as flat graphene sheets which usually have chemically modifiable-edges to which functionalisation with desired substituents may be achieved. GQDs have been used for delivery of several cancer drugs including the well-known doxorubicin; and some porphyrins used as photosensitizers for PDT [22,23]. GQDs have also been reported to be selective to cancer cells through the enhanced permeation retention (EPR) effect, where the drug loaded onto the GQDs is likely to target the cancer microenvironment over the non-cancerous one. This is because the pores on the endothelial cell membrane of the vascular system are relatively bigger at the tumor site, allowing easier entrance of NPs such as GQDs (which are larger than drugs alone) [23–25].

Additional advantages of using GQDs as delivery agents are their biocompatibility, simple synthesis methodologies and their ability to improve the solubility of some PS in aqueous media. The conjugates of Pcs with GQDs have previously been reported for sensing or for physicochemical studies [26–28]. However, Pcs-GQDs conjugates have not yet been reported for PDT, hence the subject of the current work. The PDT activity of the Pc-GQDs conjugates is investigated in vitro on human breast cancer MCF7 cell line in this work.

The aim of this work involves the preparation and photo-physicochemical characterisation of Pc and their GQDs conjugates and eventually the study of the effect the GQDs in the Pc-GQDs conjugates have on the in vitro photodynamic activity of Pcs on the human breast cancer MCF-7 cell line.

The N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Merck®. The zinc acetate (Zn(OAc)2; 99%), 1,8-diazabicyclo [5.4.0] undec-7-ene (DBU), 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), unsubstituted zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc), and dimethyl sulphate (DMS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich®. Millipore water was acquired from a Milli-Q water system (Millipore Corp, Bedford, MA, USA). Human breast adenocarcinoma Michigan cancer −7 (MCF-7) cell line was purchased from Cellonex®. The Dulbecco phosphate-buffer saline, Dulbecco’s modified eagle’s medium (DMEM) with and without phenyl-red were purchased from Lonza®. The heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS, 10% (v/v)) and the 100 μg/mL-penicillin: 100 unit/mL-streptomycin-amphotericin-B-mixture (PSA) as well as the neutral red cell proliferation reagent (WST) were all purchased from Biowest®. The Pcs 2, 3 and 4 were synthesized as previously reported in literature [29-31]. The synthesis of the metal-free form of complex 1, (2,9,16,23-tetrakis-(2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenoxy)-phthalocyanine, H2DTBPc), has been reported [32], however, the metalation with Zn is reported in this work. The preparation of 5 is also reported in this work. The GQDs used in this report are amino-functionalised GQDs, the synthesis of these GQDs is as reported in literature [33].
Equipment employed in this work may be found in the Supporting information.

To obtain complex 1, H2DTBPc (0.2 g; 0.14 mmol) was dissolved in 5 mL of dry DMF in the presence of Zn(OAc)2 (0.04 g; 0.22 mmol) and DBU (0.1 mL; 0.67 mol). The resulting mixture was left to reflux, stirring continuously under argon overnight. The reaction mixture was thereafter cooled to ambient temperature. The product was precipitated out of solution with 1:1 (v/v) methanol and water solution and collected by centrifugation at 3500 rpm for 5 min. The product was dried in an enclosed fume hood. The product was further purified and isolated using neutral alumina packed column with gradient eluents of methanol and chloroform. Yield: 75%. (log ɛ, DMSO): 678 (4.59), 610 (3.8), 354 (4.07). Anal. Calc. for C92H104N8O4Zn: C, 76.14; H, 7.22; N, 7.20%. Found: C, 76, 80; H, 7.91; N, 7.15%. 1H NMR (600 MHz, CDCl3), (δ, ppm): 9.29 (s, 7H, Ar-H), 7.97 (s, 4H, Ar-H), 7.01 (d, J = 8.52, 9H, Ar-H), 2.16 (d, J = 11.2 Hz, 12H, CH3-H), 1.50–1.17 (m, 72H, CH3H). IR[(ATR), Vmax/cm−1]: 2951 (Ar, CH stretch), 2867 (CH), 1645 (Ar, CC), 1627 (Ar, CN),1093 (COC). MALDI-TOF-MS (m/z): calculated 1451.28 g/mol. Found 1458.35 g/mol. UV/ Vis λmax (nm) (log ɛ, DMSO): 678 (4.6), 613 (4.0), 356 (4.2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quaternization of complex 4 to form 5, Scheme 2
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain complex 5, a solution of complex 4 (0.5 g, 0.32 mmol) in DMF and excess DMS was prepared and stirred for 72 h at 70 °C under argon. The mixture was then cooled to room temperature, the solid product was washed with hot acetone, and centrifuged, and thereafter continuously washed with methanol, ethyl acetate, tetrahydrofuran and chloroform, and finally dried with diethyl ether and left to dry further in fume hood. Yield: 0.36 g (76%). IR[(ATR), Vmax/cm−1]: 3044 (Ar, CH), 2796 (CH3, CH), 1603 (CN), 1478 (Ar, CC), 752 (CS). 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO-D6), (δ, ppm): 8.59–8.62 (m, 4H, Ar-H), 8.09–8.1 (m, 4H, Ar-H), 7.80–7.82 (m, 6H, Ar-H), 7.72–7.74 (d, 2H, Ar-H), 7.59–7.64 (m, 8H, Ar-H), 7.4–7.5 (d, 16H, Ar-H), 7.42 (d, 4H, Ar-H), 4.03 (s, 12H, CH3-H). UV/ Vis λmax (nm) (log ɛ, DMSO): 678 (4.4), 615 (3.8), 355 (4.3).

The method of π-π conjugation of the Pcs onto GQDs was the same for the different Pcs. The GQDs (10 mg) were dissolved in 1 mL of Millipore water and the Pc (10 mg) dissolved in 4 mL DMF in separate vials and sonicated for 4 h separately. The GQDs and the Pcs solutions were then mixed and stirred at room temperature for 48 h. The samples were thereafter washed with ethanol and collected using a centrifuge at 3500 rpm for 5 min and dried in a closed fume hood overnight.

The fluorescence (ΦF) and triplet (ΦT) quantum yields for the complexes were determined using comparative methods as described in literature [34,35]. Unsubstituted ZnPc was used as the standard where its ΦF = 0.20 [34] and ΦT = 0.65 in DMSO [35]. Quinine sulphate was used as the standard for fluorescence of GQDs, where the ΦF = 0.60 in H2SO4 [36]. Solutions for triplet quantum yield studies were deaerated for 15 min using argon, prior measurements. Calculations for the ΦF of the GQDs in the conjugates
Φ
F ( G Q D s )
c o n j u g a t e
were determined using Eq. (1) [37].
(1)
Φ
F ( G Q D s )
c o n j u g a t e
=
Φ
F ( G Q D s )
F
( G Q D s )
c o n j u g a t e
F
( G Q D s )
where
F
( G Q D s )
c o n j u g a t e
and
F
( G Q D s )
are the integrated fluorescence intensities of the GQDs in the conjugate and GQDs alone, respectively, when excited at wavelength where GQDs absorb. ΦF(GQDs) is the fluorescence quantum yield of the GQDs alone and was used as standard.

The singlet oxygen quantum yields (ΦΔ) of the complexes were determined using DPBF (and using equations which have been derived before [38]) as a singlet oxygen quencher in DMSO where the degradation of DPBF upon exposure to generated singlet oxygen was monitored at the absorption peak of DPBF at 415 nm. ZnPc was used as the standard where its ΦΔ = 0.67 in DMSO [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structural characterisation of Pcs
ce:section.ce:para: The Pc and Pc-GQDs conjugates were prepared and the structural properties investigated to confirm the formation of target complexes. For Pcs, only the structural characteristics of the new Pcs, 1 and 5, will be reported in this work. Synthetic pathways for these complexes are shown in Schemes 1 and 2. The structures of Pcs 1 and 5 were confirmed using a series of analytical techniques including mass spectrometry, NMR, elemental analysis and IR spectroscopy and agreed with the structures. Four positional isomers are formed during the cyclotetramerization reaction of phthalonitriles to form tetra-substituted Pcs, hence these complexes are represented by one fixed bond and three which are not fixed [40], Schemes 1–3.

The synthesis and structural characteristics of GQDs have been reported before [33,42]. The planar structure of GQDs, consisting of delocalized π electrons of graphene enables strong π-π interactions with other π-conjugated aromatic molecules, this type of interaction was employed in this work, as shown in Scheme 3.
The functional groups constituting the Pcs and GQDs-conjugates were confirmed by the IR spectroscopy, Fig. 2 .

The peaks of the Pcs and GQDs are present in the IR spectrum of the conjugate as shown for 3-GQDs, Fig. 2. These peaks remain intact when compared to the pristine GQDs and Pcs, since no bonds were formed nor broken. Similar trends were observed for Pcs 1, 2, 4 and 5 and their respective GQDs conjugates.

The morphology and sizes of the GQDs and the Pc-GQDs conjugates were determined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS), Fig. 3 , where the solvents were selected on a basis of solubility for sample preparation for both TEM imaging and DLS.

TEM images show that the GQDs are monodispersed in their pristine form and tend to be clustered when conjugated to the Pcs (using 1-GQDs as an example, Fig. 3). The average size for the non-conjugated GQDs determined from TEM imaging was ∼9 nm and increase upon conjugation to about ∼19 nm for 1-GQDs. The increase in size could be due to aggregation. Aggregation is probably due to interactions between the Pcs on adjacent NPs via π-π stacking since Pcs are known for their π-π stacking [41]. The sizes from DLS are listed in Table 1. The sizes determined from DLS are usually slighter larger in comparison to those determined by TEM, since DLS tend to be skewed toward larger particles [43]. This is the case for 1-GQDs, but not for GQDs alone, Fig. 3C and D. There is an increase in the DLS sizes following conjugation, for reasons given above.

The Raman spectra of the GQDs and Pc-GQDs consist of G-band (sp2 hybridized) tangential mode and d-disorder band (sp3 hybridized) breathing mode. The Fig. 4 shows the Raman spectrum of 4-GQDs where the G band occurs at 1525 cm−1 and the d-band at 1331 cm−1. The D band for 4-GQDs is shifted to higher wavenumbers compared to the pristine GQDs. The shifts in the Raman frequencies are often indicative of strong π-electron interactions in hybrid materials [44].

The ratio of the intensity of the bands (ID/IG) predicts the degree of disorder (sp3) introduced on the carbon sheet (sp2) of GQDs upon conjugation [25,26]. The ID/IG ratio of the GQDs is 0.08. Upon conjugation, the ID/IG shows an increased for all the complexes (Table 1). This may be due to the layering of Pcs comprising sp3 geometries on their substituents.

The loading of complexes 1–5 onto the GQDs was investigated following literature methods [17]. This involves comparing the Q band absorbance intensity of the Pc in the conjugate with that of the initial Pc before the conjugation. The loading of the Pc is lower for Pcs with larger substituents (1-GQDs, 4-GQDs¸and 5-GQDs). The loading may have been affected by the bulky substituents, which may prevent many Pc molecules being loaded.

The synthesized Pc and their respective conjugates were further characterised based on their UV-vis absorption trends. The GQDs absorb (λmax = 365 nm) father from the Q band of the Pc, Fig. 1C. Upon conjugation, the ‘B-band’ increases relative to that of the Pc’s alone. This may serve as a confirmation for conjugation of Pc to GQDs, with the increase in the absorbance at the blue-region being due to the GQDs absorption in the conjugate. There is a slight enhancement in the vibronic band as shown in Fig. 1C, for 2-GQDs compared relative to 2. However, the Q band maintains its narrow behaviour confirming monomeric behaviour. The Q band maxima of the Pcs slightly blue shifts upon conjugation Table 1. This effect has been previously reported and is indicative of interaction of the dye (e.g. Pc) adsorbed onto GQDs [45].

The photophysical properties of the complexes including the fluorescence (ΦF), triplet (ΦT) and singlet (ΦΔ) quantum yields were determined in DMSO. Most of the parameters have been reported for the Pc 2,3,4 [30,46,47] and the GQDs [42]. However, those of Pc 1, 5 and all the conjugates, will all be reported in this work.

The UV-vis absorption spectrum of 1 is similar to the respective excitation spectrum and is the mirror image of the emission spectrum as shown in Fig. 5 A. This confirms that the same molecule that is absorbing is the same molecule emitting. There is a slight decrease in the ΦF of the Pcs (when excited where Pcs absorb) in the conjugates for 3-GQDs and 4-GQDs and insignificant changes for the rest of the conjugates, Table 2 . However, a drastic decrease in the ΦF of the GQDs (when excited where GQDs absorb) of the conjugates is observed. The decrease in the ΦF of GQDs in the Pc-GQDs may be due to the occurrence of several processes including photon induced electron transfer (PET), inter-charge transfer (ICT) which occurs between functional groups on the structures, or Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) among other processes [48]. It is important to note that the B bands of the Pcs are located where GQDs absorb this is likely to affect the ΦF values hence the values are for comparative purpose only.

The fluorescence lifetimes (τF) of the Pcs and Pc-GQDs conjugates were determined using time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC), Fig. 6 . The fluorescence lifetimes of the Pc complexes generally decrease on conjugation even though there were no changes in ΦF in some cases, Table 2. τF and ΦF values are expected to change in unison. Increase or decrease in the fluorescence lifetime may depend on the geometry or distance between the metal and Pc molecule [49]. The fluorescence lifetimes of the Pc-GQDs conjugates when excited at wavelengths were the GQDs absorb, 354 nm, drastically decrease to negligible values (<0.01 ns, hence not added to Table 2) for all the conjugates. This decrease may be due to FRET among other inter and intramolecular processes.

FRET is based on the phenomenon of energy transfer between the emitting donor to the acceptor, in this case the GQDs as the donor and Pc acceptor in the conjugate [48]. However, for FRET to occur there must be a spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the donor (in this case, GQDs) and the absorbance spectrum of the acceptor (Pcs). There is little overlap between the spectra, Fig. 5B. Hence the decrease in ΦF and ΦF values of the GQDs, could be due to non-FRET processes such as changes in surface defects [50]. Fig. 5B shows emission of the 2 in 2-GQDs when exciting where GQDs absorb at 354 nm, however Pcs alone also absorb in this region (B band) with the emission spectrum shown as in insert in Fig. 5B. Hence the stimulated emission and FRET cannot certainly be confirmed.

The triplet quantum yields (ΦT) and lifetimes (τT) are listed in Table 2. The ΦT values increased upon conjugation corresponding to the decrease in fluorescence quantum yields. The triplet decay curve, Fig. 7 , obeyed second order kinetics, typical of MPc complexes at high concentration, due to triplet–triplet recombination [51].

Shortening of triplet lifetimes is expected with increase in triplet quantum yields [52], however, a lengthening of lifetimes is observed in all cases in Table 2 in the presence of GQDs. This could be due to the protection of Pcs afforded by GQDs.

The singlet quantum yields ΦΔ of the Pc and Pc-GQDs were determined using DPBF as a singlet oxygen quencher. The degradation of DPBF was monitored at 415 nm after irradiation at 20 s intervals in DMSO, Fig. 8 .

The Pcs alone and in the conjugates demonstrate photostability during photoactivation, for example Pc 1 as shown in Fig. 8. The Q band of the Pc did not degrade upon irradiation over time, while DPBF degraded. It is expected that ΦΔ will follow the same trend as ΦT in that the former should increase where the latter increases. This is not the case, as shown in Table 2. In all cases, the ΦΔ values of the Pcs decrease in the presence of GQDs. The observed decrease of the ΦΔ values of the Pc-GQDs could be due to the screening effect caused by the GQDs, which could have prevented the interaction of the excited triplet state of the conjugates and the ground state molecular oxygen [53].

The importance of GQDs is still on the delivery of the Pc to cancer cells as stated in the introduction. Based on the photophysical properties obtained for the Pc and the Pc-GQDs in comparison, it is justifiable that the conjugates are potentially applicable for in vitro cytotoxicity studies.

The PDT activities of the Pcs and Pcs-GQDs on human breast cancer MCF-7 were studied. The compounds were inoculated into 96-well plates containing the MCF-cells, at different concentrations 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 μg/mL. The cells were thereafter incubated at 37 °C for 24 h prior irradiation. Experimental details can be found in the Supporting Information. Most of the Pcs and Pcs-GQDs are not water soluble, thus 1% DMSO (in cultured media) was used to dissolve the Pc complexes and their conjugates. For the in vitro PDT studies, four controls were also tested including 1% DMSO (Fig. 9 ), culture media, GQDs and dark toxicity. The Pcs alone or the conjugates did not show dark toxicity, Fig. 9A.

Not all the complexes/conjugates reached 50% cell destruction during PDT with the tested concentrations. A summary on the activity of the complexes/conjugates, according to cell viability after PDT at the highest concentration used, 100 μg/ mL, is shown in Table 3 .

There is no obvious trend between the ΦΔ and the PDT activity for both the Pc and the Pc-GQDs. For example, there is an increase in cell viability (decrease in PDT activity) for 1-GQDs, 2-GQDs, and 5-GQDs compared to Pcs alone, corresponding to decrease in ΦΔ values. However, for 3-GQDs and 4-GQDs, there is a decrease in cell viability (increase in PDT activity) compared to Pcs alone, even though ΦΔ values decreased. It should be noted that the cytocidal effect of the different photosensitizer is dependent on several factors including cell type, cellular uptake, and localization [54]. Thus, the effect of GQDs in enhancing PDT activity depends on the substituents on the Pc ring, with the neutral Pcs containing S substituents showing improved PDT activity compared to Pcs alone. As stated above, the importance of GQDs may still be based on the delivery of the Pc to cancer cells even where there is no improvement in PDT activity of Pcs in the presence of GQDs. Furthermore, GQDs did not exhibit cytotoxic effects on the human breast cancer cell MCF-7 cell line in the dark nor light when administered in the absence of the Pc. It could therefore be assumed that the cytotoxic effects are only due to the photoactivity of the Pc as the photosensitizer when irradiating with light of wavelength where only the Pc absorb. It is, however, noteworthy that the complexes do not linearly eradicate the cancer cells with an increase in the concentration dose. From Fig. 9B, Pc 4 initially shows more PDT activity in comparison with 4-GQDs (from 5 to 20 μg/mL). At higher concentrations, 40–100 μg/mL the 4-GQDs surpass 4. In contrast, 5-GQDs showed better PDT activity compared to 4-GQDs until 60 μg/mL. Previous studies have shown that cationic anti-cancer compounds are more effective in comparison with their neutral/uncharged parent [55]. This can be explained by the better retention of the cationic drug on the cancer cells owing to the negative membrane potential of the cancer cell [55,56]. The initial relatively low PDT activity of 5-GQDs at lower concentrations compared to 4-GQDs may be due to low concentration of Pc in the conjugate since the loading of 4 on the GQDs was better compared to 5, thus efficiency is only witnessed at higher concentrations for 5-GQDs.

The cell studies were carried forward once the growth reached 100% confluency. Fig. 10
A shows live cells after treatment with 5-GQDs and incubation in the dark after 24 h. The confluency of the cells were relatively high ∼100% when compared to those that were irradiated in the presence of 5-GQDs, Fig. 10B, where the cell viability, by confluency, was greatly reduced. There are slight changes in the morphology of cells after PDT, which appear slightly rounder. These cells may be the “near-death” cells, which will be round once they become loose and lift from the growth media as a result of PDT cytocidal effects.

We studied the photo-physicochemical properties and in vitro photodynamic activity on the human breast cancer MCF-7 cell lines of a series of Zn(II)Pc derivatives and how these properties may be affected upon conjugation to GDQs by π-π interaction. A decrease in the ΦF (insignificant in some cases) and the consequent increase in the ΦT was observed for GQDs conjugates. The ΦΔ however, was reduced for the GQDs compared to the non-conjugated Pc. This was suspected to be caused by the screening effect from the GQDs, inhibiting facile energy transfer to the oxygen from the triplet state. The Pc-GQDs conjugates do however demonstrate PDT activity on the human breast cancer MCF-7 cell lines upon irradiation. Moreover, factors including the structure of the substituents on the Pcs, loading and photo-physicochemical properties of the Pc and GQDs play an important role in the PDT activity of the Pc-GQDs complexes and should be considered in the design of nano-medicine platforms.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) Innovation and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University. Kind acknowledgement to Professors Kobayashi N and Kimura M from Shinshu University, Japan, for training on synthesis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ica.2019.01.012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: M. J. Stillman, T. Nyokong, The Porphyrin Handbook, A.B.P. Lever, C.C. Leznoff Edition, Volume 1, Chapter 3, New York, NY, (1989) 133-289.
